title: Rollout History and Targeted Rollback
description:
## Rollout History and Targeted Rollback

Every `kubectl set image` or `kubectl apply` on a Deployment creates a new **revision**. You can inspect history and roll back to any specific revision — critical when a rollback must skip intermediate bad versions.

**Your task:**

A Deployment named `versioned-app` has been updated through three image versions. It is now running a broken image.

1. Inspect the rollout history: `kubectl rollout history deployment/versioned-app`
2. Examine revision 2 in detail to confirm it used `nginx:1.24`
3. Roll back **specifically to revision 2** (not just the previous revision)

```bash
# View all revisions:
kubectl rollout history deployment/versioned-app

# Inspect a specific revision:
kubectl rollout history deployment/versioned-app --revision=2

# Roll back to a specific revision:
kubectl rollout undo deployment/versioned-app --to-revision=2
```

hints.title: View rollout history
hints.body: kubectl rollout history shows all recorded revisions. Each image change creates a new revision.

hints.title: Roll back to a specific revision
hints.body: Use --to-revision=N to target a specific revision rather than just the last one.

commands.description: Deployment image is rolled back to nginx:1.24
Deployment has at least 1 ready replica
commands.command: kubectl get deployment versioned-app -o jsonpath='{.status.readyReplicas}' 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^$' || echo 0
